naming_inspiration.description: Named for her paternal great-grandmother Theodosia Pierpont Walter (H00868) — preserving the Pierpont-Walter great-grandmother's name across three generations into a Hayden-line granddaughter.

narrative_blocks.header: Died one day before twenty-first birthday
narrative_blocks.body: Theodocia Pierpoint Walter Hayden was born August 20, 1817 and died August 19, 1838 — one day before her twenty-first birthday, in New Haven Connecticut. She had married Nathaniel Hayden about thirteen months earlier on July 6, 1837. The compressed marriage-motherhood-death cycle across thirteen months captured the period's vulnerability of young women in the postpartum period.

Theodocia Pierpoint Walter Hayden was named for her paternal great-grandmother Theodosia Pierpont Walter — preserving the great-grandmother's name across three generations of women. The Pierpont-Walter-Hayden name chain carried family memory of the eighteenth-century North Haven Pierpont line into a nineteenth-century Hartford-County Hayden household.

narrative_blocks.header: One son who would die at twelve
narrative_blocks.body: Theodocia's only documented child William Walter Hayden was born August 7, 1838 — twelve days before her death. The son would live only twelve years before drowning in the Connecticut River in 1850. The Walter-Hayden line through Theodocia thus produced no surviving direct descendants past 1850.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for her paternal great-grandmother Theodosia Pierpont Walter (H00868) — preserving the Pierpont-Walter great-grandmother's name across three generations into a Hayden-line granddaughter.

narrative_blocks.header: Died one day before twenty-first birthday
narrative_blocks.body: Theodocia Pierpoint Walter Hayden was born August 20, 1817 and died August 19, 1838 — one day before her twenty-first birthday, in New Haven Connecticut. She had married Nathaniel Hayden about thirteen months earlier on July 6, 1837. The compressed marriage-motherhood-death cycle across thirteen months captured the period's vulnerability of young women in the postpartum period.

Theodocia Pierpoint Walter Hayden was named for her paternal great-grandmother Theodosia Pierpont Walter — preserving the great-grandmother's name across three generations of women. The Pierpont-Walter-Hayden name chain carried family memory of the eighteenth-century North Haven Pierpont line into a nineteenth-century Hartford-County Hayden household.

narrative_blocks.header: One son who would die at twelve
narrative_blocks.body: Theodocia's only documented child William Walter Hayden was born August 7, 1838 — twelve days before her death. The son would live only twelve years before drowning in the Connecticut River in 1850. The Walter-Hayden line through Theodocia thus produced no surviving direct descendants past 1850.

narrative_blocks.header: Gale & Hayden silver firm partner, NYC 1846-50
narrative_blocks.body: Nathaniel Hayden was partner with William Gale in the New York City silversmith firm Gale & Hayden from 1846 to 1850. Gale & Hayden silver appears in many important public and private collections including the Winterthur Museum in Wilmington Delaware. The Gale & Hayden presentation ewer at Winterthur was won by Henry F. Du Pont's grandfather as an agricultural award in 1849 — making it a known piece in American silver history.

narrative_blocks.header: Earlier Charleston SC partner with William Gregg
narrative_blocks.body: Before the NYC partnership, Nathaniel Hayden was a prominent jeweler in Charleston South Carolina in partnership with William Gregg from 1838 to 1843. After he left for New York, his brother H. Sidney Hayden replaced him in the Charleston partnership 1846-52, with the shop at 232 King Street. The career arc moved Hayden between two of the most important American silver-and-jewelry centers of the era.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost wife and only child to early death
narrative_blocks.body: Nathaniel married Theodocia Pierpoint Walter on July 6, 1837 — she died August 19, 1838 at age twenty, one day before her twenty-first birthday, about thirteen months after the marriage. Their only son William Walter Hayden drowned in 1850 at age twelve. Nathaniel outlived his wife by thirty-seven years and his son by twenty-five — he died February 23, 1875 in Hayden, Hartford County Connecticut at age sixty-nine.

narrative_blocks.header: Drowned bathing in Connecticut River at twelve
narrative_blocks.body: William Walter Hayden drowned on August 23, 1850 while bathing in the Connecticut River near Windsor, Hartford County Connecticut — age twelve years and sixteen days. The summertime drowning ended the Walter-Hayden direct descent line: his mother Theodocia had died at twenty just twelve days after his birth in 1838; his father Nathaniel survived him by twenty-five years but had no further marriages or children documented.

narrative_blocks.header: Born twelve days before his mother died
narrative_blocks.body: William Walter Hayden was born August 7, 1838 — twelve days before his mother Theodocia Pierpoint Walter Hayden died on August 19, 1838 at age twenty. The compressed birth-and-maternal-death sequence reflected the period's postpartum mortality risk for young first-time mothers. William was raised by his father Nathaniel and Hayden grandparents in the Hayden Hartford-County household.

narrative_blocks.header: Died Charleston South Carolina age thirty
narrative_blocks.body: William Walter Jr. died October 6, 1817 in Charleston, South Carolina at age thirty — four days short of his thirty-first birthday. His body was returned to New Haven Connecticut for burial at Grove Street Cemetery. The Charleston-to-New Haven body return tracks the established New England merchant-and-professional travel patterns of the period, when business interests took young New Englanders to southern coastal cities.

narrative_blocks.header: Three young children at his death
narrative_blocks.body: William Jr. left three young children at his 1817 death: Ann K. (age 5), Lavinia (already deceased as an infant in 1815), and Theodocia Pierpoint (born about a year before his death). His wife Nancy Kirby continued raising the surviving children before her own death in 1830 at age thirty-nine.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Louisa died thirty days after marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Theodosia's daughter Louisa Walter married Timothy Bishop on April 3, 1800 — and died on May 3, 1800, just thirty days after the marriage. Louisa was twenty. The compressed marriage-and-death cycle within a single calendar month captured the period's vulnerability to acute disease across all socioeconomic groups, even within the Pierpont-Walter household at New Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-granddaughter named Theodocia Pierpont in her honor
narrative_blocks.body: A generation after Theodosia's 1806 death, her son William Walter's daughter named her own daughter Theodocia Pierpont Walter Hayden (born August 20, 1817) — honoring the Pierpont great-grandmother through both first name and middle name. The naming carried the Theodosia-and-Pierpont identity forward across three generations of women in the family.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandson William Walter Hayden drowned in childhood
narrative_blocks.body: Theodosia's great-grandson William Walter Hayden — son of Theodocia Pierpont Walter Hayden — drowned on August 2, 1850 in Windsor Connecticut at age twelve. The cascading family losses — daughter at twenty in 1800, great-granddaughter at twenty in 1838, great-great-grandson at twelve in 1850 — touched four consecutive generations of the Walter-Hayden line.

narrative_blocks.header: Born North Haven 1758, died New Haven 1806
narrative_blocks.body: Theodosia Pierpont was born February 22, 1758 in North Haven Connecticut and died July 15, 1806 in New Haven Connecticut at age forty-eight. She married William Jay Walter on November 17, 1777 in North East Village, New Haven — he survived her by eighteen years, dying April 19, 1824. Her five documented children were Joel, Louisa, Lovina, Jerry, and William Jr.
